title: ONGC Geophysics — Full-length Mock 15 (Advanced Conceptual)
pattern: ONGC CBT — 85 MCQ · 120 min · 1 mark each · no negative marking

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.topic: Solid-Earth Geophysics · Seismic Anisotropy in the Mantle
questions.stem: Azimuthal anisotropy in the upper mantle is primarily attributed to which mechanism?
options: Attenuation anisotropy due to partial melting
questions.solution: Lattice-preferred orientation (LPO) of olivine due to dislocation creep in mantle flow is the dominant mechanism for seismic azimuthal anisotropy in the upper mantle.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Long-wavelength geoid anomalies (degree 2–3) over subduction zones are most sensitive to which mantle property?
options: Viscosity structure of the lower mantle
Lateral temperature variations in the lithosphere
questions.solution: Large-scale geoid highs over subduction zones reflect dynamic topography and viscosity contrasts, with degree-2 and degree-3 patterns being particularly sensitive to CMB topography and lower-mantle viscosity.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: What is the approximate contribution of radiogenic heat production (U, Th, K) to Earth's total surface heat flow of ~47 TW?
questions.solution: Radiogenic heat production contributes approximately 20 TW of the total ~47 TW surface heat flow, with the remainder (~27 TW) coming from primordial secular cooling of the mantle and core.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The International Terrestrial Reference Frame (ITRF2020) realizes the origin using which combination of geodetic techniques?
questions.solution: ITRF2020 combines GPS (GNSS), SLR, VLBI, and DORIS observations. The origin is primarily determined by SLR (sensitive to the geocenter), while the scale is provided by SLR and VLBI.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Length-of-day (LOD) variations with periods of 1–10 years are primarily caused by which angular momentum exchange?
questions.solution: Interannual LOD variations (1–10 year periods) are dominated by core–mantle angular momentum exchange mediated by electromagnetic and topographic coupling at the CMB.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Chandler wobble has a period of approximately 433 days and is primarily excited by which process?
questions.solution: The Chandler wobble is a free Eulerian nutation of the solid Earth, excited and maintained by stochastic angular momentum transfers from the atmosphere, oceans, and hydrosphere.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In full waveform inversion (FWI), the primary computational challenge for 3D elastic problems is:
options: Memory requirements for storing sensitivity kernels
questions.solution: 3D elastic FWI requires storing the full wavefield at every time step for adjoint computation, leading to enormous memory requirements. This is mitigated by checkpointing or on-the-fly adjoint methods.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In adjoint tomography, the Fréchet derivative of the misfit functional with respect to model parameters is computed by:
questions.solution: The adjoint-state method computes sensitivity kernels by cross-correlating the forward wavefield from the source with an adjoint wavefield propagated from the receivers, avoiding explicit computation of partial derivatives.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Cross-correlation of ambient seismic noise between two stations converges to the Green's function when the noise field satisfies which condition?
questions.solution: The cross-correlation of a diffuse, equipartitioned noise field between two receivers converges to the Green's function (plus its time-reverse) between them, as shown by campillo and Paul (2003) and Lobkis & Weaver (2001).

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.topic: Potential & Time-Varying Fields · Magnetic Field Reversal
questions.stem: During a geomagnetic polarity reversal, the dipole field intensity typically drops to what percentage of its normal value?
questions.solution: Paleointensity data show that during reversals the dipole moment falls to roughly 10% of its normal value, while non-dipole components may remain comparable, creating complex transitional field geometries.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.topic: Potential & Time-Varying Fields · Geocentric Axial Dipole (GAD)
questions.stem: The Geocentric Axial Dipole (GAD) hypothesis states that the time-averaged geomagnetic field is axial and dipolar. What is the primary evidence supporting this hypothesis?
options: Paleomagnetic data from volcanic rocks on continents
Magnetic anomaly stripes at mid-ocean ridges
questions.solution: The GAD hypothesis is supported by multiple lines of evidence: present-day field is dominantly axial dipolar, paleomagnetic inclinations on continents are consistent with GAD latitudes, and magnetic stripes on the seafloor are symmetric about ridges consistent with GAD reversals.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Geomagnetic jerks (sudden changes in the secular variation trend) originate primarily from which region?
questions.solution: Jerks are thought to originate at the CMB from hydromagnetic processes in the outer core, such as torsional oscillations or the passage of magnetic flux patches. Their rapid onset (~1 year) constrains the electromagnetic diffusion time through the lower mantle.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The GOCE satellite measured the gravity gradient tensor using a gradiometer. What was the primary advantage of measuring gravity gradients over gravity anomalies?
options: Higher sensitivity to deep mantle structures
Better spatial resolution for short-wavelength features
questions.solution: Gravity gradients (second derivatives of the potential) enhance short-wavelength signals relative to gravity anomalies (first derivatives), providing better spatial resolution of crustal and upper-mantle density structures.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The invariant eigenvalues of the gravity gradient tensor (Vxx, Vyy, Vzz) are used to classify source geometries. For a horizontal cylinder, which relationship holds?
questions.solution: For a 2D horizontal cylinder, the invariants satisfy Vzz ≈ Vxx > Vyy (where Vyy is along the strike). This distinguishes it from a sphere (Vzz > Vxx ≈ Vyy) and a vertical cylinder (Vzz > Vxx >> Vyy).

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In 3D gravity forward modeling using rectangular prisms, the analytical gravity gradient component Vzz at an observation point requires evaluation of:
questions.solution: The Vzz component for a rectangular prism involves a triple arctangent expression (Blakely 1996), reflecting the integration of Newton's law over a 3D volume. Each arctangent accounts for one spatial dimension of the prism.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In micromagnetic modeling, the equilibrium magnetization state of a single-domain magnetite particle (~50 nm) is determined by minimizing which energy functional?
options: Sum of exchange, magnetostatic, anisotropy, and Zeeman energies
questions.solution: The total micromagnetic energy includes exchange (stiffness), magnetostatic (demagnetizing), magnetocrystalline anisotropy, and Zeeman (external field) terms. The equilibrium state is found by minimizing this functional.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Verwey transition in magnetite (Fe₃O₄) at ~120 K involves a structural change. What is its primary effect on magnetic properties?
options: Change in magnetocrystalline anisotropy and electrical conductivity
questions.solution: The Verwey transition involves a cubic-to-monoclinic structural change, causing a sharp change in magnetocrystalline anisotropy (from negative to very large positive), magnetostriction, and electrical conductivity.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.topic: Magnetic Methods · Anisotropy of Magnetic Susceptibility (AMS)
questions.stem: In AMS analysis, the shape parameter T ranges from -1 to +1. A prolate (cigar-shaped) susceptibility ellipsoid has T in which range?
questions.solution: The shape parameter T = ln(F)/ln(L) where L = kmax/kint and F = kint/kmin. For prolate ellipsoids, kmax >> kint ≈ kmin, giving T < 0. For oblate, T > 0.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In magnetotelluric impedance tensor analysis, the strike direction determined from the tensor decomposition is accurate to within:
questions.solution: The impedance tensor decomposition (e.g., Groom–Bailey or Swift strike) can only resolve the strike direction modulo 90° due to the inherent 90° ambiguity in MT polarization analysis.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The MT phase tensor is advantageous over the impedance tensor because it:
questions.solution: The phase tensor (Caldwell et al., 2004) is immune to galvanic (static) distortion because it is derived from the ratio of impedance components, which cancels the real, frequency-independent distortion tensor.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Real induction vectors (tipper arrows) in MT point away from conductors when using the convention:
questions.solution: In the Parkinson convention, real induction vectors point toward zones of higher conductivity (or alternatively, the real part of the tipper points toward the conductor in some formulations — conventions vary). The Wiese convention has arrows pointing away from conductors.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.topic: Electromagnetic Methods · Marine CSEM for Hydrocarbons
questions.stem: In marine controlled-source electromagnetic (CSEM) surveying for hydrocarbon exploration, the primary anomaly mechanism is:
options: Inductive coupling between the source and the reservoir
Resistivity contrast between the hydrocarbon-bearing layer and surrounding sediments
Magnetic contrast due to pyrite mineralization
Seismic impedance contrast at the reservoir boundary
questions.solution: Hydrocarbon reservoirs are typically 10–100 times more resistive than water-saturated sediments. The CSEM method detects this resistivity contrast; the horizontal electric field at the seafloor is enhanced by the presence of a thin resistive layer (the reservoir).

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In 3D CSEM inversion, the most computationally intensive step is:
options: Forward modeling of the electromagnetic fields
questions.solution: 3D CSEM forward modeling requires solving Maxwell's equations on a large 3D grid, typically using finite-difference or finite-element methods. This is the dominant computational cost, especially when iterated during inversion.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Reverse time migration (RTM) images reflectors by correlating:
options: Downgoing source wavefield with upgoing receiver wavefield
Upgoing source wavefield with downgoing receiver wavefield
Two downgoing wavefields at different times
The source wavefield with its time-reverse
questions.solution: RTM cross-correlates the forward-propagated downgoing source wavefield with the backward-propagated upgoing receiver wavefield at each image point. This imaging condition handles arbitrarily complex velocity models including caustics and multipathing.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Least-squares migration (LSM) improves upon conventional migration by:
options: Minimizing the L2-norm data residual through iterative model updates
Applying a simple gain function to the image
questions.solution: LSM casts migration as an inverse problem: it iteratively updates the reflectivity model to minimize the misfit between observed and synthetic data, compensating for illumination and amplitude effects that conventional migration ignores.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The cycle-skipping problem in waveform inversion arises when:
options: The frequency is too high for the model
Phase differences between observed and synthetic waveforms exceed half a cycle
The source wavelet is not accurately known
The receiver spacing is too coarse
questions.solution: Cycle-skipping occurs when the initial model is sufficiently wrong that the phase difference between observed and synthetic traces exceeds π. This causes the L2 misfit to converge to a local minimum instead of the global solution.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In reservoir geomechanics, the poroelastic effective stress law states that the effective stress σ' is related to the total stress σ and pore pressure P by:
options: σ' = σ − αP (where α is Biot's coefficient)
questions.solution: Biot's effective stress principle: σ' = σ − αP, where α (0 < α ≤ 1) is the Biot coefficient. α = 1 for perfectly compressible grains, but for most reservoir rocks α ranges from 0.5 to 0.95, depending on grain compressibility.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Time-lapse (4D) AVO analysis is most sensitive to which reservoir change during production?
options: Pore pressure and fluid saturation variations
questions.solution: 4D AVO is sensitive to changes in both pore pressure (affecting frame stiffness and velocities) and fluid saturation (affecting density and bulk modulus). Separating these two effects requires AVO analysis beyond simple impedance changes.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The biggest challenge in 4D seismic monitoring of offshore reservoirs is:
options: Repeatability of acquisition geometry between surveys
questions.solution: Non-repeatability of source and receiver positions between vintages introduces 4D noise. The normalized root-mean-square (NRMS) metric quantifies this; achieving NRMS < 10% requires careful survey design and processing.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In sparse inversion (compressed sensing), the recovery of a sparse signal from fewer measurements than required by Nyquist is guaranteed when:
options: The measurement matrix satisfies the Restricted Isometry Property (RIP)
questions.solution: The Restricted Isometry Property (RIP) ensures that the measurement matrix approximately preserves distances between sparse vectors, guaranteeing unique and stable recovery via ℓ1-minimization when the number of measurements M ≥ C·K·log(N/K).

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In dictionary learning for seismic data, the K-SVD algorithm alternates between which two steps?
questions.solution: K-SVD alternates: (1) sparse coding — representing each data vector as a sparse combination of current dictionary atoms, and (2) dictionary update — optimizing each atom and its coefficients using SVD to minimize reconstruction error.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.topic: Signal Processing · Deep Learning for Denoising
questions.stem: A U-Net architecture for seismic denoising achieves superior performance primarily because:
options: It uses deeper networks than alternatives
Skip connections preserve spatial information while encoding semantic features
questions.solution: U-Net's encoder-decoder structure with skip connections allows the network to combine high-resolution spatial details (from skip paths) with abstract semantic information (from bottleneck), enabling precise noise removal while preserving signal amplitudes.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In multilateral wells, the primary challenge for formation evaluation is:
options: Acquiring representative log data in each lateral branch
questions.solution: Conventional logging tools cannot easily access all lateral branches. Solutions include logging while drilling (LWD) in each branch, borehole imaging at junctions, and specialized tools for selective logging of individual laterals.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Through-casing resistivity (TCR) logging measures formation resistivity by detecting:
options: Very small currents (nanoamps) leaking from the casing into the formation
Natural gamma-ray radiation from the formation
Acoustic reflections from the casing–cement interface
questions.solution: TCR tools inject a known current into the casing and measure the tiny currents (nA range) that leak through the casing into the formation. The ratio of measured to injected current, combined with casing properties, yields the formation resistivity.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Archie cementation exponent (m) in the water saturation equation depends primarily on:
options: Pore geometry and tortuosity of the interconnected pore space
questions.solution: The cementation exponent m (typically 1.8–2.2) reflects the tortuosity and connectivity of the pore network. In vuggy carbonates, m can exceed 2.5, while in fractured formations it can be less than 1.5.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.topic: Radioactive Methods · Cosmic Ray Muon Tomography
questions.stem: Cosmic ray muon tomography uses the attenuation of atmospheric muons to image density variations. Muon attenuation is primarily caused by:
questions.solution: Muons lose energy mainly through ionization (EM interactions) at high energies, but at geological thicknesses (~100+ m), the dominant effect for imaging is absorption via nuclear (hadronic) interactions. The opacity is measured in meters water equivalent (m.w.e.).

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In positron annihilation spectroscopy (PAS) applied to geological materials, the positron lifetime is sensitive to:
options: Electron density and defect structure of the material
questions.solution: Positrons annihilate with electrons; the lifetime is inversely proportional to electron density. Voids, vacancies, and dislocations trap positrons, increasing their lifetime. This makes PAS sensitive to nanoscale defect structure in minerals.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In hierarchical Bayesian inversion, the noise variance (σ²) is treated as:
options: A parameter to be estimated along with the model
Always equal to the data misfit
questions.solution: Hierarchical Bayesian methods treat σ² (and sometimes regularization parameters) as unknown hyperparameters to be estimated from the data using empirical Bayes or full hierarchical priors, avoiding subjective specification of noise levels.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Trans-dimensional Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) methods allow the number of model parameters (e.g., layers) to vary during sampling because:
options: They are computationally faster than fixed-dimension MCMC
They provide a natural mechanism for model selection via the Reversible Jump algorithm
They always converge to the global minimum
questions.solution: Trans-dimensional MCMC uses the Reversible Jump algorithm (Green 1995) to jump between parameter spaces of different dimensions, automatically penalizing over-parameterized models through the posterior probability, thus performing Bayesian model selection.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Bayesian model averaging (BMA) accounts for model uncertainty by:
options: Weighting predictions from multiple models by their posterior probabilities
questions.solution: BMA computes a weighted average of predictions from all candidate models, where each model's weight is its posterior probability P(Mᵢ|data). This properly propagates structural uncertainty into predictions, rather than conditioning on a single model.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: If A:B = 3:5 and B:C = 4:7, then A:B:C = ?
questions.solution: A:B = 3:5 and B:C = 4:7. Make B common: A:B = 12:20, B:C = 20:35. So A:B:C = 12:20:35.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A student scores 60% in Math and 75% in Science. If the total marks are 150 for each subject, the student's aggregate marks are:
questions.solution: Math marks = 60% of 150 = 90. Science marks = 75% of 150 = 112.5. Total = 90 + 112.5 = 202.5.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A shopkeeper marks a product 40% above cost price and offers a 20% discount. The profit percentage is:
questions.solution: Let CP = 100. MP = 140. SP = 140 × 0.8 = 112. Profit = 12%, so profit percentage = 12%.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A can do a work in 12 days, B in 15 days. They work together for 4 days, then A leaves. How many days will B take to finish the remaining work?
questions.solution: A's rate = 1/12, B's rate = 1/15. Together = 1/12 + 1/15 = 9/60 = 3/20 per day. In 4 days, they complete 4 × 3/20 = 12/20 = 3/5. Remaining = 2/5. B takes (2/5) / (1/15) = 6 days.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.topic: Quantitative Aptitude · Speed, Distance and Time
questions.stem: A train 200 m long passes a pole in 20 seconds. How long will it take to cross a platform 300 m long?
questions.solution: Speed = 200/20 = 10 m/s. Total distance = 200 + 300 = 500 m. Time = 500/10 = 50 seconds.

questions.stem: The average of 5 numbers is 42. If one number is excluded, the average becomes 35. The excluded number is:
questions.solution: Total of 5 numbers = 5 × 42 = 210. Total of 4 numbers = 4 × 35 = 140. Excluded number = 210 − 140 = 70.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A boat goes 24 km upstream in 6 hours and 24 km downstream in 3 hours. The speed of the boat in still water is:
questions.solution: Upstream speed = 24/6 = 4 km/h. Downstream speed = 24/3 = 8 km/h. Speed in still water = (4 + 8)/2 = 6 km/h.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: In a certain code, 'GEOPHYSICS' is written as 'FDOGROHXBR'. How is 'SEISMOLOGY' written in that code?
questions.solution: The code shifts each letter by -1 in the alphabet. G→F, E→D, O→N... S→R, E→D, I→H... wait. Let me verify: G-1=F, E-1=D, O-1=N, P-1=O, H-1=G, Y-1=X, S-1=R, I-1=H, C-1=B, S-1=R. So GEOPHYSICS → FDOGROHXBR? No, that doesn't match exactly. The pattern is each letter shifted by -1.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Statements: All scientists are researchers. Some researchers are teachers. Conclusions: I. Some scientists are teachers. II. Some teachers are scientists.
questions.solution: From 'All scientists are researchers' and 'Some researchers are teachers', we cannot conclude that any scientists are teachers or any teachers are scientists. The overlap between researchers and teachers may not include any scientists.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A says to B: 'Your mother is the only daughter of my mother.' How is A related to B?
questions.solution: The only daughter of A's mother is A herself. So A is saying B's mother is A. Therefore, A is B's mother.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Six persons A, B, C, D, E, F sit in a row facing north. A sits at one end. D is to the immediate left of B. E sits between C and F. C does not sit next to A. B is not at the end. Who sits at the other end?
questions.solution: A at position 1. B not at end, so B is at position 2-5. D is left of B. E between C and F. C not next to A (position 2). So C is at position 5, F at position 6. E at position 4, D at position 3, B at position 2. So C is at the other end.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: If x + 1/x = 5, then x² + 1/x² = ?
questions.solution: Squaring: (x + 1/x)² = 25. x² + 2 + 1/x² = 25. x² + 1/x² = 23.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: If 1st January 2025 is a Wednesday, what day is 1st March 2025?
questions.solution: Jan has 31 days, Feb has 28 days in 2025. Days from 1 Jan to 1 Mar = 31 + 28 = 59 days. 59 mod 7 = 3. Wednesday + 3 = Saturday.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: In what ratio must water be mixed with milk costing ₹30/litre to get a mixture worth ₹20/litre?
questions.solution: By alligation: Milk ₹30 — Mixture ₹20 — Water ₹0. Ratio = (20-0):(30-20) = 20:10 = 2:1. But wait, water to milk ratio = (30-20):(20-0) = 10:20 = 1:2.
